title: Cobogó
summary: Local-first spatial workspace for Markdown. It started as a renderer, then folders became part of the idea.
origin: SpatialMD was the first step: a way to write diagrams and visual notes in plain text. That version is dead. Cobogó is the idea that survived it.

image.alt: Cobogó showing a dark spatial workspace with folders and markdown pages arranged on a canvas.
image.caption: A folder scene in the current Kotlin Multiplatform build. Still just local files underneath.

lede: I thought the problem was rendering Markdown. Then editing pulled the document onto a surface. Then the same question started applying to folders.

sections.title: Files should not always feel like lists.
body: Markdown is good because it survives tools. Most Markdown apps still make the work feel like a tree, a sidebar, and a pile of tabs.
Cobogó keeps the files local, then gives folders and documents a spatial surface where position can carry meaning too.

sections.title: A renderer became a workspace.
body: The current build has a shared parser and renderer, folder scenes, draggable cards, source editing, search, image previews, live refresh, and persisted layouts.
The desktop app is the real testbed. Android and iOS exist as targets, but the public promise is not there yet.

sections.title: Active, but not announced.
body: It is past the pure demo stage and still before the clean public story. The next work is packaging, sharper examples, and deciding what the first real use case should be.

title: Farol
summary: A testnet Cardano wallet for people who want the wallet to talk to their own node.

image.alt: Farol wallet summary screen with a dark interface, wallet balance, and preprod testnet badge.
image.caption: A desktop-first wallet UI over a self-hosted cardano-wallet backend. Preprod only.

lede: Farol means lighthouse. The product line is simple: your wallet, your node. No cloud custody story, no mainnet promise, no fake balance screen.

sections.title: A calm UI over real wallet plumbing.
body: A lot of wallet prototypes either hide the node completely or expose every backend detail as debug noise.
Farol tries the middle ground: a restrained interface over real cardano-wallet endpoints, with the dangerous parts labeled and gated.

sections.title: Desktop first, testnet first.
body: It talks to cardano-wallet and a local cardano-node socket. Wallet list, restore, balances, addresses, transactions, fee estimates, send flow, staking, settings, explorer links, and Farol Connect are wired through real flows.

sections.title: The safety boundary is part of the product.
body: Farol is preprod and testnet only for now. That is not a footnote. It is the line that keeps the app honest while the interaction model is still being shaped.

title: Pinote
summary: A bike game where the crashes are part of the design process.

image.alt: Pinote gameplay showing a red motorcycle jumping across low buildings in a stylized Brazilian city.
image.caption: A frame from the public gameplay video. The landing is usually the interesting part.

lede: Pinote is a motorcycle game built in Bevy. The point is not a pristine racing sim. It is the moment when a jump almost works and the crash is funny enough to keep.

sections.title: Feel first, explanation later.
body: This is where I tune things directly: bike mass, wheelies, jumps, air control, ragdolls, traffic, and the rhythm of a city that keeps giving you bad ideas.

sections.title: A little city to make mistakes in.
body: The prototype uses Bevy, Avian physics, procedural city generation, dynamic chunks, traffic, scoring, replay hooks, and a small public gameplay page at cotinga.games.

sections.title: Playable, rough, still alive.
body: The best screenshots are usually accidents. That is useful: when the accident is fun, the game is pointing at what it wants to become.

title: linconvidal.com
summary: This site. A quiet archive for photos, software, games, and notes.

image.alt: linconvidal.com homepage with large typography, a photograph, and links to photography, software, games, and notes.
image.caption: The site became the container for the rest: photographs first, but not photographs only.

lede: I wanted a quieter site again. Photographs are still the center, but not the whole thing. Software, games, and notes also need somewhere to land when they are worth keeping around.

sections.title: One address for the things that should not disappear.
body: The old shape was too narrow. I wanted a place that could hold photographs, small notes, and pages for things I have built without turning everything into a pitch deck.

sections.title: Static on purpose.
body: It is an Astro site with photography pages, notes, software and games timelines, and individual work pages that can grow as each thing accumulates screenshots, links, and context.

sections.title: The site is also a work in progress.
body: This page is a test case for the whole site: show work clearly, keep the voice human, and avoid pretending that unfinished things are finished.
